questions.questionText: A disease with high prevalence but low incidence is most likely:
questions.optionB: Chronic with long duration (prevalence = incidence × duration; high prevalence with low incidence indicates prolonged disease course)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Prevalence = Incidence × Duration. Why correct: If prevalence is high but incidence is low → disease has LONG DURATION (chronic disease, e.g., diabetes, hypertension). P = I × D. Why A wrong: Acute fatal disease → short duration → low prevalence despite incidence. Why C wrong: Readily curable → short duration → low prevalence. Why D wrong: Rare means low prevalence. Contagious may increase incidence. Clinical relevance: Prevalence useful for planning healthcare resources. Incidence useful for studying etiology and evaluating prevention. Exam trap: If a new effective treatment increases survival (longer duration), PREVALENCE increases even if incidence stays same. Cure reduces prevalence. Memory aid: 'P = I × D: Prevalence Inflates with Incidence and Duration.' Reference: Park – Epidemiology.

questions.questionText: In a case-control study, the measure of association between exposure and disease is:
questions.optionB: Odds ratio (ratio of odds of exposure in cases vs controls — used because incidence cannot be directly calculated in case-control studies)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Case-control studies use odds ratio; cohort studies use relative risk. Why correct: Case-control design starts with disease status (cases vs controls) and looks backward for exposure → cannot directly calculate incidence → cannot get relative risk. Odds ratio approximates RR when disease is RARE (<10%). Why A wrong: Relative risk (RR) is used in COHORT studies (follow exposed and unexposed → compare incidence). Why C wrong: Attributable risk = difference in incidence (cohort studies). Why D wrong: Incidence rate cannot be calculated from case-control studies. Clinical relevance: OR = (a×d)/(b×c) in 2×2 table. OR > 1 = positive association. OR = 1 = no association. OR < 1 = protective. Exam trap: In rare diseases, OR ≈ RR. In common diseases, OR overestimates RR. Memory aid: 'Case-control = Can't calculate incidence = Use Odds Ratio.' Reference: Park – Epidemiology.
questions.conceptTag: Case-control odds ratio cohort relative risk

questions.questionText: The gold standard study design for establishing causality between an intervention and outcome is:
questions.optionB: Randomized controlled trial (RCT — experimental design with randomization, blinding, and control group minimizes bias and confounding)
questions.explanation: Core concept: RCT is the strongest evidence for causality (Level 1 evidence). Why correct: RCT features: randomization (eliminates selection bias, distributes confounders), blinding (eliminates observer/subject bias), control group (comparison), prospective. Eliminates most types of bias → highest internal validity for causal inference. Why A wrong: Case-control — retrospective, prone to recall bias, shows association not causation. Why C wrong: Cross-sectional — snapshot, cannot establish temporal relationship. Why D wrong: Ecological — population-level data, ecological fallacy. Clinical relevance: Hierarchy of evidence: systematic review/meta-analysis > RCT > cohort > case-control > cross-sectional > case series > expert opinion. Exam trap: RCT is NOT possible/ethical for harmful exposures (e.g., smoking → cancer) → use cohort for these. Memory aid: 'RCT = Real Causation Tested (gold standard for intervention).' Reference: Park – Research Methodology.

questions.questionText: Positive predictive value (PPV) of a screening test is influenced by:
questions.optionB: Prevalence of disease in the population (higher prevalence → higher PPV; lower prevalence → more false positives → lower PPV)
questions.explanation: Core concept: PPV depends on disease prevalence — high prevalence increases PPV. Why correct: PPV = TP/(TP+FP). When prevalence increases, more true positives in the tested population → PPV increases. With low prevalence, even highly specific tests generate many false positives relative to true positives → PPV drops. Why A wrong: Sensitivity affects NPV more than PPV. Why C wrong: Specificity affects PPV, but prevalence is the MOST IMPORTANT external factor. Why D wrong: Test methodology determines sensitivity/specificity, not directly PPV. Clinical relevance: A test with 99% sensitivity and 99% specificity in a disease with 1% prevalence → PPV only 50%! In 0.1% prevalence → PPV drops to ~9%. Exam trap: Sensitivity and specificity are INTRINSIC (not affected by prevalence). PPV and NPV are EXTRINSIC (affected by prevalence). Memory aid: 'PPV = Prevalence Primarily Varies it.' Reference: Park – Screening.

questions.questionText: An outbreak of food poisoning occurs among wedding guests. The most appropriate study design to investigate the cause is:
questions.optionB: Retrospective cohort study (all attendees are the cohort — compare attack rates between those who ate specific food items vs those who didn't)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Outbreak investigation uses retrospective cohort of all exposed individuals. Why correct: All guests form a defined cohort. Compare attack rates among those who consumed specific food items vs those who didn't. The food item with highest relative risk/attack rate = likely cause. Why A wrong: RCT — cannot ethically randomize food exposure in an outbreak. Why C wrong: Cross-sectional gives prevalence but doesn't efficiently compare exposures. Why D wrong: Double-blind trial — experimental, not applicable to outbreak investigation. Clinical relevance: Steps of outbreak investigation: confirm diagnosis, verify existence, define case, count cases, orient data (time-place-person), develop hypothesis, test hypothesis (cohort/case-control), implement control measures, communicate findings. Exam trap: If cohort is SMALL and population at risk not defined → case-control study instead (compare food history of cases vs healthy guests). Memory aid: 'Wedding outbreak: Who ate What → Retrospective cohort.' Reference: Park – Outbreak Investigation.
questions.conceptTag: Outbreak investigation retrospective cohort attack rate
